story:
At St Agatha’s, everyone loved Dr Ravi Maharaj, the only cardiologist in the hospital. He had a hand that steadied a shoulder and a voice that could quiet fear. When he couldn’t save a failing heart, he kept vigil to the end. Yet for a man who treated hearts all day, he knew remarkably little about his own.

When I first met him, he was in his forties, bustling through wards like a portly bowling ball, trailing apologetic juniors. Work filled every hour: extra clinics, letters, emergencies. It had been drilled into him in India—no time to pause, no time for life—and he had become exceptionally skilled. But I sensed his busyness was also a shield. He wore the same shirts, his moustache went untrimmed, his waist widened. He did not look kindly at the man in the mirror, and in his culture, reaching his age unmarried was shameful. His romantic confidence lay in his boots.

We met properly over Mr Patel, an elderly, cheerful survivor of heart attacks and stents, now swollen and breathless. Ravi was already at the bedside, speaking Hindi, advocating for chicken korma from Hiren’s shop as incentive to get better. I bristled at first, then saw it wasn’t a power move. He simply cared. Mr Patel wanted more time—for a grandchild’s birth, a graduation, a world tour—and I told him he had more than he thought.

At Christmas, fate seated me beside Ravi at the work dinner. We talked deep into the night—about Medicine’s limits, the system’s cracks. Something unspoken passed between us, childless and aware that time was slipping. I saw a longing in him to be himself, and a promise that I could be the one.

Our love was charmingly bradycardic: steady rhythm, slow beats. He had never kissed anyone. The first time I held his hand, he recoiled, then muttered that hand-holding was for teenagers. I wore him down. Soon he leaned into me on cobblestones, pressed shy, bristly kisses when no one watched. He insisted we keep it secret. We arrived at work separately, traded glances at the back of meetings, and hid behind sunglasses on a Pride float, laughing while terrified a patient would spot him on the news.

Then came his parents’ visit. I thought it was our chance. The closet door beckoned. He said he couldn’t tell them—“shameful,” he whispered—and asked that we stop seeing each other until they left. I refused to keep pretending our love meant nothing. He said it again: “I can’t.” We ended.

For a decade we orbited the same hospital, polite and clinical. I watched his moustache silver, his body change, his title swell to Chief Medical Officer. He never married. I went to Pride alone and hoped he saw.

We collided again at Christmastime, of all places, outside an Indian takeaway. “Chicken korma,” he said, and admitted he had made a mistake. His parents were gone. He asked for a second chance. The stem had once been cut; the roots remained. We grew again.

In our fifties, love felt different. He bore the hospital’s burdens and missed patients. We had money, flights, hotels, freedom. We almost settled into watching Pride on TV until older couples pulled us back into the street. Then the call came: Director of Cardiology in Kashmir. I offered to follow and hide. He refused to go back into the shadows.

“I want to hold your hand,” he said. “I want to kiss you without stares. I want to marry, to adopt, to raise a child who carries our kindness forward. I won’t let you go this time.”

For all his expertise in cardiology, it took Ravi fifty-six years to qualify as a specialist in the heart.
